shortDescription: Surface laser vision correction in Korea that reshapes the cornea without cutting a flap, suited to thin corneas and active lifestyles.

sections.overview: LASEK (laser-assisted subepithelial keratectomy) is a surface-based form of laser vision correction used to treat myopia, hyperopia, and astigmatism. Unlike LASIK, which lifts a thicker corneal flap, or SMILE, which removes a tissue lenticule through a small incision, LASEK works directly on the surface of the cornea. The surgeon loosens the thin outer epithelial layer with a dilute alcohol solution, gently moves it aside, reshapes the underlying corneal tissue with an excimer laser, and then repositions or allows the epithelium to regenerate. Because no permanent flap is created, LASEK preserves more of the deeper stromal tissue. This makes it a frequent recommendation for people whose corneas are too thin for LASIK, those with steeper or irregular corneal shapes, and people in physically demanding jobs or contact sports where a corneal flap could theoretically be dislodged. The trade-off is a longer surface-healing period: vision tends to fluctuate during the first days and weeks and may not fully sharpen until several weeks after surgery. South Korea has become a well-known destination for vision correction of all types, with high-volume Seoul eye centers offering LASEK alongside LASIK and SMILE, transparent pre-operative testing, and structured follow-up. For international patients from Africa and elsewhere, LASEK in Korea combines surface-ablation safety with competitive pricing and experienced ophthalmology teams.
sections.candidacy: LASEK is most often suggested for adults with stable refractive error who are not ideal candidates for flap-based surgery. Good candidates typically have thinner-than-average corneas, where removing a LASIK flap would leave too little residual stromal tissue, or have steep, flat, or slightly irregular corneal shapes. People with active lifestyles, military or law-enforcement roles, or contact sports often prefer LASEK because there is no corneal flap that could be displaced by impact. Candidacy generally requires being at least eighteen, having a refractive prescription that has been stable for roughly a year, and having healthy eyes free of significant disease such as advanced glaucoma, keratoconus, or untreated dry eye. Pregnancy and breastfeeding can temporarily alter vision and usually mean postponing surgery. Anyone considering LASEK should expect a thorough screening, including corneal topography, corneal thickness (pachymetry) measurement, pupil size, tear-film evaluation, and a detailed refraction. These tests determine whether LASEK, LASIK, SMILE, or implantable lenses is the safest match. Reputable Korean clinics treat this assessment as a gatekeeping step and will recommend an alternative, or advise against surgery, when LASEK is not appropriate for a particular patient.
sections.procedure_steps: LASEK is an outpatient procedure performed under topical anesthetic eye drops, so no needles or general anesthesia are needed, and most patients are in the surgical suite for only a short time per eye. First, the surgeon places a lid holder to keep the eye open and applies numbing drops. A dilute alcohol solution is then applied briefly to the surface of the cornea to loosen the thin epithelial layer. This softened epithelial sheet is gently moved to one side rather than being cut away as a flap. With the surface exposed, an excimer laser delivers a precisely calculated pattern of pulses that reshape the underlying corneal tissue to correct the refractive error; eye-tracking technology helps keep the laser aligned if the eye moves. After ablation, the surgeon repositions the epithelial layer or allows it to regrow, then places a soft bandage contact lens over the cornea to protect the healing surface and improve comfort. The eye is rinsed and lubricated, and the same steps are repeated for the second eye when both are treated the same day. The active laser portion usually lasts under a minute per eye. Patients rest briefly and then go home the same day with detailed drop schedules and protective instructions.
sections.recovery_timeline: Recovery from LASEK is more gradual than LASIK or SMILE because the surface epithelium must regenerate. A bandage contact lens is usually worn for several days while the new epithelial layer grows back, after which the lens is removed at a follow-up visit. During the first three to five days many patients notice light sensitivity, watering, a gritty foreign-body sensation, and blurred or fluctuating vision; these symptoms typically ease as the surface heals. Functional vision often improves within the first week, but crisp, fully stable vision can take several weeks to settle, sometimes around the third or fourth week, and minor fluctuations may continue for a month or longer. Surgeons commonly prescribe antibiotic and anti-inflammatory drops plus lubricating tears, and following the drop schedule closely is important. Patients are usually advised to avoid rubbing the eyes, swimming, saunas, dusty environments, and eye makeup for a period, and to wear sunglasses outdoors. For medical travelers, this slower healing curve matters: planning enough time in Korea for the bandage-lens removal and at least one or two early follow-up checks before flying home is sensible, and clinics can advise on a realistic stay length.
sections.cost_breakdown: LASEK in South Korea is generally priced competitively compared with many Western countries. Reported ranges for surface-based laser vision correction in Seoul commonly fall in the area of roughly 1,000 to 3,200 US dollars for both eyes, depending on the clinic, the specific laser platform used, the complexity of the prescription, and whether premium or customized (wavefront-guided) treatment is selected. Some standard LASEK packages are quoted at the lower end, in the region of about 750 to 1,400 US dollars for both eyes, while more advanced customized procedures and well-known high-volume centers sit higher in the range. These prices frequently bundle the pre-operative diagnostic workup, the surgery itself, the bandage contact lenses, and a set of post-operative follow-up visits and medications, though the exact inclusions vary by clinic and should be confirmed in writing. International patients should also budget separately for flights, accommodation for the recovery and follow-up period, interpretation or coordination services if used, and any additional drops or sunglasses. Because LASEK is typically considered elective, it is usually paid out of pocket. Requesting an itemized quote that lists exactly what is and is not included, and confirming the currency, helps avoid surprises and makes it easier to compare offers fairly.
sections.why_korea: South Korea has built a strong international reputation for laser vision correction, supported by very high surgical volumes, modern equipment, and experienced ophthalmologists concentrated in Seoul and other major cities. The country recorded more than one million foreign patients in 2024, reflecting the scale and maturity of its medical-tourism sector. For international patients, an important reassurance is the accreditation framework overseen by the Korea Health Industry Development Institute (KHIDI), which runs the official Medical Korea program and the accreditation scheme for hospitals and clinics serving foreign patients. KHIDI-aligned standards reference internationally recognized frameworks such as Joint Commission International and World Health Organization patient-safety goals, and a number of Korean hospitals also hold JCI accreditation directly. Dedicated vision-correction centers in Seoul perform large numbers of LASEK, LASIK, SMILE, and implantable-lens procedures each year, which helps build surgical expertise and refine outcomes. Many of these centers operate international patient departments that assist with language support, scheduling, and follow-up coordination. When choosing a provider, international patients are encouraged to use official resources such as the Medical Korea portal to confirm a clinic's accreditation status, verify that surgeons are properly licensed, and ensure that pre-operative screening and post-operative follow-up are clearly explained before committing.

faqs.q: How is LASEK different from LASIK and SMILE?
faqs.a: LASEK is a surface procedure: the surgeon loosens and moves aside the thin outer epithelial layer, reshapes the cornea with a laser, and lets the surface heal. LASIK creates a thicker corneal flap that is lifted and replaced, while SMILE removes a small tissue lenticule through a tiny incision. LASEK creates no permanent flap, so it preserves more deep corneal tissue but heals more slowly.

faqs.q: Who is a good candidate for LASEK?
faqs.a: LASEK is often recommended for adults with stable vision who have thinner corneas, steep or irregular corneal shapes, or active lifestyles where a corneal flap could be a concern, such as contact sports or military roles. A full eye exam including corneal thickness and topography is needed to confirm suitability and rule out conditions like keratoconus or significant dry eye.

faqs.q: How long does recovery from LASEK take?
faqs.a: LASEK heals more slowly than LASIK because the surface epithelium must regrow. A bandage contact lens is usually worn for several days, with light sensitivity and blurriness common in the first few days. Useful vision often returns within a week, but fully sharp, stable vision can take several weeks to settle.

faqs.q: How much does LASEK cost in South Korea?
faqs.a: Reported prices commonly range from roughly 1,000 to 3,200 US dollars for both eyes, with some standard packages quoted lower and advanced customized treatments higher. Quotes often include pre-operative testing, the surgery, bandage lenses, and follow-up visits, but inclusions vary, so it is best to request an itemized quote and confirm the currency.

faqs.q: How long should international patients stay in Korea for LASEK?
faqs.a: Because the surface heals gradually, patients should plan to stay long enough for the bandage contact lens to be removed and to attend at least one or two early follow-up checks before flying home. The exact length depends on healing and the clinic's protocol, so confirm a recommended stay with the provider in advance.
